concepts.title: Biodiversity Hotspots
concepts.description: Biodiversity hotspots are regions with exceptionally high numbers of species and endemics, but are also under severe threat from human activities. Examples include Madagascar and the Galapagos Islands. These regions are crucial for conservation efforts.

concepts.title: Primary Producers
concepts.description: Primary producers are organisms that produce their own food through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis. Examples include plants, algae, and some bacteria. They form the base of the food web in an ecosystem.

concepts.title: Energy Flow in Ecosystems
concepts.description: Energy flows in one direction from producers to consumers in a food chain. It is lost at each trophic level due to respiration and other processes. Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next.

concepts.title: Trophic Levels
concepts.description: Mnemonics: 'Producer Producers Produce Plenty of Protoplasm' to remember the order: Producers (P1) → Primary Consumers (P2) → Secondary Consumers (P3) → Tertiary Consumers (P4)

concepts.title: Symbiotic Relationships
concepts.description: Students often confuse mutualism and commensalism. Mutualism is a relationship where both organisms benefit, while commensalism is a relationship where one organism benefits and the other is neither harmed nor helped. Commensalism is not a one-way benefit.

concepts.title: Ecological Pyramids
concepts.description: Ecological pyramids show the relationships between organisms in an ecosystem. There are three types: Pyramid of Number, Pyramid of Biomass, and Pyramid of Energy. Each type represents a different aspect of ecosystem structure.

concepts.title: Decomposers
concepts.description: Decomposers are organisms that break down dead or decaying organisms, releasing nutrients for other organisms to use. Examples include bacteria, fungi, and some insects. They are essential for recycling nutrients in an ecosystem.

concepts.title: Food Chains and Food Webs
concepts.description: Food chains show a linear relationship between organisms, while food webs show a more complex network of relationships between organisms in an ecosystem.
